# Questions in Livestream 134

Live Stream #134: Home Sweet Home

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction |
|---:|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=371" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">6:11</a> | If Rameses II's firstborn died around 1254 BC, why is that not the date of the Exodus? | He said the answer depends on the starting chronology for Rameses II; newer chronologies can push the approximate Exodus date earlier, around 1263-1265 BC. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=541" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:01</a> | Why do most ancient Near Eastern texts and literature never achieve final redaction or canonization, but instead have multiple versions? | He pointed to scribal transmission: hand copying, updating, and copying errors create textual variants before later ideas of fixed manuscript forms develop. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=863" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:23</a> | Are there books on Genesis he recommends besides Walton? | He could not recommend a specific overview off the top of his head and said he usually goes to Walton or to commentaries. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=1001" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:41</a> | Does Walton overstate his case about ancient Near Eastern material and functional origins? | He said Genesis and origins are so complex that any scholar may eventually overstate a case, though he did not generally accuse Walton of doing so. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=1412" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:32</a> | Why are there more stories of Christian biblical scholars losing faith, such as Bart Ehrman, than secular scholars coming to faith? | He argued the shift usually happens while people are students, before they become scholars, and that both conversion and deconversion can occur during that formation period. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=1717" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:37</a> | How should Jude's use of non-canonical sources such as the Assumption of Moses and 1 Enoch be understood? | He said Jude uses familiar cultural material to support a narrow point, not to declare those sources inspired or fully historically authoritative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=1996" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:16</a> | How should Genesis 6:1-4 be interpreted, and does the relative clause in 6:4 refer to "those days" or "afterward"? | He treated the passage cautiously and emphasized following the wording and structure of the text rather than importing later speculative readings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=2840" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:20</a> | Is it possible that retrojection and name updating were used for certain places and people in the Bible, explaining why archaeological evidence is sometimes hard to find? | He said name updating and polemical renaming do happen, but they only explain some cases and do not play a huge role in archaeological gaps. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=3258" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:18</a> | How would he respond to the objection that Yahweh originally came from a natural volcanic phenomenon on a mountain? | He said there is no evidence for the theory, no clear active Late Bronze Age volcano in the relevant area, and even if the association existed it would not disprove Yahweh's identity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=3738" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:18</a> | Would Joseph have been educated in Egyptian religion or considered a priest in Egypt as vizier? | Probably. He said a vizier would likely have had education in Egyptian religion and at least some priestly titles, since Egyptian officials commonly held such titles. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=3915" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:15</a> | Has he read Forgotten Luther, and what does he think of progressive Christians using Luther to support modern movements such as economic justice? | He had not read the book, but said using Luther for modern social-justice agendas sounded like quote-mining and an abuse of Luther's historical context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=4355" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:35</a> | Given what happened during his father's reign, why was Merneptah not hesitant to attack Israel? | He said Merneptah's campaign was aimed at Gezer, not Israel; Israel and the Hurrians were incidental encounters during a narrow campaign. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=4678" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:58</a> | How should Jeremiah 46:25-26 be interpreted if Babylon never conquered Egypt? | He said the evidence points more to Babylonian raids than a full conquest, including likely attacks in the eastern Delta and temple looting, which fits the prophecy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=5157" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:25:57</a> | Was Yahweh a low-level god before Israelite worship? | He said the evidence is too limited to know Yahweh's status in any earlier Midianite or related setting; origins hypotheses remain speculative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=5222" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:02</a> | What are his thoughts on the historicity of the Book of Esther? | He thinks Esther is historical and argued that many objections overstate the case by importing modern expectations or missing Persian-period context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=5636" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:56</a> | What cities are safe to travel in Egypt today? | He said many cities are relatively safe, but warned strongly against dangerous regions such as Sinai. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=5937" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:57</a> | How did Islam take over Egypt? | He said Egypt was taken by military conquest in the seventh century, with Muslim forces taking major cities such as Memphis and Alexandria. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=6046" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:46</a> | Is there a good biblical Bronze Age role-playing game? | He did not know of one he could recommend, but said a Bible-based RPG module could theoretically be made using a system such as Dungeons & Dragons. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=6376" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:46:16</a> | Did Greek mythology, especially demigods, influence the idea that the Nephilim were angel hybrids in the Second Temple period or modern day? | He thought Greek mythology may have been minor at most; he located the main source in Second Temple Jewish speculation after earlier contexts had faded. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=6574" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:34</a> | How radically different were First and Second Temple period theologies, and how much influence did the Babylonian exile have? | He said the exile had significant influence on Second Temple theology and helped produce many of the differences. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=6903" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:03</a> | If Jude quotes Enoch, is he asserting that Enoch truly prophesied the quoted prophecy? | Not necessarily. He said Jude is making a narrow argument with the quoted material, not endorsing all of Enoch. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=7148" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:59:08</a> | Will Egypt collapse from overpopulation? | He did not think collapse was imminent, saying Egypt was producing and exporting more food than before, though inflation and prosperity brought other pressures. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=7478" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:04:38</a> | Could Job 37-38 be written as a play dramatizing real events, given the storm language? | He said "play" was the wrong genre; Job is poetic compressed language using storm imagery to convey God's majesty and power. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=7790" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:09:50</a> | What are his thoughts on progressive dispensationalism? | He was not a fan of dispensationalism, said progressive dispensationalism is better than the traditional form, but argued salvation has always been by grace and the system is unnecessary. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=8323" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:43</a> | If Disney bought the rights to the Bible and made many productions from it, which obscure characters should get their own series? | He answered mostly in banter, mentioning Rahab and Balaam's donkey while criticizing Disney's habit of aggressive copyright control. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=8614" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:23:34</a> | Why was demotic script unsuitable for later Coptic? | He said demotic was hard to read, ligature-heavy, and restricted to scribes, while Coptic needed an alphabetic script usable by a wider Greek-speaking and lay audience. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=8943" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:03</a> | Why personally believe in Yahweh if the odds are that he was a Mesopotamian byproduct like other deities? | He rejected the premise, saying Yahweh worship is attested before major Mesopotamian influence and is better traced to Bronze Age or southern origins than Mesopotamia. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=9148" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:32:28</a> | How old do we know Yahweh worship is compared with possible Mesopotamian adaptation? | He said Yahweh worship is at least ninth century BC and likely earlier, with proposed evidence from names, inscriptions, and possible Shasu references, though some evidence remains debated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=9348" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:48</a> | Was he saying Early Bronze Age? | No. He clarified that he meant Late Bronze to early Iron I, not Early Bronze Age. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=9420" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:37:00</a> | Which of Hess's works discusses early second-millennium Yahweh names? | He was not sure it had been published and remembered hearing the paper at an ASOR meeting around 2017 or 2018. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=9599" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:39:59</a> | Does he have any unique theories in Egyptology? | He said most of his Egyptological work is conventional, but his view that Osiris's green or black coloring reflects rot and decay rather than fertility is more controversial. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=10171" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:31</a> | How much money would it take to get a school to make him a professor? | He explained endowed chairs, estimating a major-school chair might require several million dollars while smaller or foreign schools could require much less. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=10601" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:56:41</a> | Did he talk with David Wood? | Only briefly. He passed him at the convention center, and IP wanted to facilitate more conversation later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=10691" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:58:11</a> | Did Noah's flood need to be water, or could it symbolize a chaotic period, and could the ark be a fortress? | He said the ark is a chest, not a fortress, and read the flood through cultic and ritual categories of sacred purification and preparation for the new world. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=11101" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:05:01</a> | Does he believe the flood was global or local? | He said he is not comfortable deciding either way and thinks the global/local debate is largely beside the passage's cultic point. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=11934" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:18:54</a> | Did Plato get the Atlantis story from Egypt? | He said we do not know where Plato got it from, but Egypt does not preserve an Atlantis story or equivalent that would explain it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=12107" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:21:47</a> | What are Zechariah 12:10 and 14:1-4 about; do they describe real Israel being attacked and Jesus returning? | He agreed that the passage concerns a future attack on real Israel and Christ's return, not the church replacing Israel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=12734" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:32:14</a> | Does the subtle theme of sacred and profane space across Scripture seem divinely inspired? | He agreed, saying that theme is woven through the arc of Scripture from creation onward. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=12817" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:33:37</a> | Does the Stargate reference really taste like a Mars bar? | He did not know the reference at first, then recognized it as a Stargate/pop-culture question and said he had not watched Stargate in a while. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=12977" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:36:17</a> | What does he think about academic pronunciations, especially in Egyptian, and why? | He said scholarly pronunciations are useful conventions for dead languages, but cautioned against speculative reconstructions and against conflating different phases of Egyptian. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=13519" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:45:19</a> | When will he shave off the goatee? | Never, he joked; his producer would not let him shave it off. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=13940" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:52:20</a> | What dissertation ideas for Genesis 1-3 or other biblical passages would give valuable insights and help the church? | He suggested many narrow Genesis 1-3 topics, including death in the garden, the serpent, Genesis 3 judgment, formless and void, vegetation, rivers, stones, nakedness, enmity, and curse language. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=15066" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:11:06</a> | How would ancient Egyptians react to modern pop-culture depictions of mummies resurrecting as monsters? | He compared it to people digging up graves and using remains disrespectfully, so Egyptians would likely find it offensive. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=15275" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:14:35</a> | Why did the whole family have to remain inside during the Passover rather than just mark the door and leave the firstborn at home? | He answered in terms of communal guilt: the household needed to be under the sign together. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=15490" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:18:10</a> | Has he seen any more ghosts at work? | No, not since changing jobs; the earlier workplace was the one with the strange experiences. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=15702" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:21:42</a> | Is the bronze serpent in Numbers 21:9 related to the caduceus or the rod of Asclepius? | Probably not directly. He said many cultures use serpents as healing symbols because of skin-shedding, so the examples share a broader theme rather than a direct relationship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=15943" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:25:43</a> | How should ancient Near Eastern context be used in biblical theology, and where does it go too far? | He said context is valuable for interpreting the text, but it goes too far with cosmetic parallels, parallelomania, or assuming similarity proves direct borrowing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=16450" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:34:10</a> | Would he agree that Deuteronomy is a treaty? | No. He said Deuteronomy has treaty language and is a law code, but he thinks the treaty pattern fits parts of Exodus and the Sinai covenant better than Deuteronomy. |
